title: Why Banks Are Still Losing the Real-Time Payments Race to Fintech
summary: Alex Johnson argues that the real bottleneck for RTP and FedNow adoption isn't network participation — it's banks' unwillingness to invest in the ERP connectors and workflow automation that corporate clients actually need before they'll switch from ACH.
businessImpact: Johnson's core argument lands hard for treasury product teams: corporate clients aren't asking for real-time payments in the abstract, they're asking for SAP and Oracle plug-ins that make the switch invisible. Banks that treat real-time rails as a payment product rather than a workflow integration will keep losing volume to fintechs who've already built the connectors.
businessTakeaway: ERP integration is the product. Real-time rails are the infrastructure.

title: Tokenized Deposits Are Not a Stablecoin — And That Distinction Is Everything
summary: Simon Taylor breaks down why the market conflates bank-issued tokenized deposits with non-bank stablecoins, and why the regulatory distinction the Fed is drawing creates a three-year window for banks to build programmable money products that non-banks legally cannot.
businessImpact: Taylor's framing is strategically important for treasury teams: the regulatory moat around bank-issued tokenized deposits is real and time-limited. Banks that treat this as a 2027 roadmap item are making a material strategic error. The programmable liquidity use case — automated intraday sweeps, conditional payments, repo settlement — is available to banks now and to their non-bank competitors later.
businessTakeaway: First-mover banks in tokenized deposits capture the programmable liquidity relationship, which is stickier than any payment rail.

title: Community Banks Are FedNow's Biggest Opportunity — and Biggest Risk
summary: Kiah Haslett reports on how community banks' FedNow send-side activation gap is creating a two-tier real-time payments ecosystem where large banks deliver the full value proposition while community banks sit on receive-only status, accelerating deposit and treasury client drift.
businessImpact: Haslett identifies the hidden competitive risk: community and regional banks that are FedNow receive-only are delivering a degraded experience to treasury clients who want bidirectional real-time payments. The activation gap is not a technical problem — it's a prioritization and vendor-dependency problem that is measurably accelerating treasury client attrition to larger institutions.
businessTakeaway: Receive-only FedNow participation is a competitive liability for any bank serving corporate treasury clients.
